coredata.dc:description: An innovative aptasensor incorporating MoS2-modified bicolor quantum dots and a portable spectrometer, designed for the simultaneous detection of ochratoxin A (OTA) and aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) in corn was developed. Carbon dots and CdZnTe quantum dots were as nano-donors to label OTA and AFB1 aptamers, respectively. These labeled aptamers were subsequently attached to MoS2 receptors, enabling fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). With targets, the labeled aptamers detached from the nano-donors, thereby disrupting the FRET process and resulting in fluorescence recovery. Furthermore, a portable dual-mode fluorescence detection system, complemented with customized python-based analysis software, was developed to facilitate rapid and convenient detection using this dual-color FRET aptasensor. The developed host program is connected to the spectrometer and transmits data to the cloud, enabling the device to have Internet of Things (IoT) characteristics. Connected to the cloud, this IoT-enabled device offers convenient and reliable fungal toxin detection for food safety.

head.ce:title: Self-designed portable dual-mode fluorescence device with custom python-based analysis software for rapid detection via dual-color FRET aptasensor with IoT capabilities

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An innovative aptasensor incorporating MoS2-modified bicolor quantum dots and a portable spectrometer, designed for the simultaneous detection of ochratoxin A (OTA) and aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) in corn was developed. Carbon dots and CdZnTe quantum dots were as nano-donors to label OTA and AFB1 aptamers, respectively. These labeled aptamers were subsequently attached to MoS2 receptors, enabling fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). With targets, the labeled aptamers detached from the nano-donors, thereby disrupting the FRET process and resulting in fluorescence recovery. Furthermore, a portable dual-mode fluorescence detection system, complemented with customized python-based analysis software, was developed to facilitate rapid and convenient detection using this dual-color FRET aptasensor. The developed host program is connected to the spectrometer and transmits data to the cloud, enabling the device to have Internet of Things (IoT) characteristics. Connected to the cloud, this IoT-enabled device offers convenient and reliable fungal toxin detection for food safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mycotoxins, secondary metabolites produced by fungi during crop growth, storage, and transportation, pose a significant threat to food safety (Hassan et al., 2023). Notably, aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) and ochratoxin A (OTA) are the two most commonly co-occurring mycotoxins found in agricultural products (Li et al., 2023). AFB1 is classified by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) as a group one carcinogen, while OTA belongs to group two (Wang et al., 2024; Zhou, Sun, et al., 2023). However, mycotoxins often coexist in agricultural products, and their growth can proliferate rapidly under favorable temperature and humidity conditions (Qian et al., 2023). This complexity necessitates a more comprehensive approach beyond single-mycotoxin testing for accurate food toxin contamination assessment (Song et al., 2019). Currently, chromatographic methods, such as liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), are primarily employed for the detection of multiple mycotoxins (Ma et al., 2019; Vaclavik et al., 2013). While these techniques offer high accuracy, they are also associated with drawbacks like being time-consuming, costly, and technically intricate. Given these limitations, the development of a portable, cost-effective, and rapid method for the detection of multiple mycotoxins is paramount for effective food safety monitoring and on-site inspections.

Recently, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) based fluorescent sensors have garnered significant attention for the detection of mycotoxins (Fan et al., 2021; Luo et al., 2020; Yin et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2021). FRET, or förster resonance energy transfer, relies on the efficient transfer of energy between a donor and an acceptor in fluorescent sensors. Quantum dots, renowned for their exceptional photostability, favorable biocompatibility, and cost-effectiveness, are often employed as donors in FRET-based sensors. For instance, Tian et al. designed a graphene quantum dot-based fluorescence sensor for OTA detection using FRET (Tian et al., 2018). Similarly, Liu et al. developed a quantum dot/porphyrin-based nanosensor utilizing self-assembled zinc porphyrin for OTA detection (Liu et al., 2020). The sensitivity of fluorescence sensors hinges heavily on the choice of donor and acceptor. Hence, selecting the appropriate FRET pattern receptors is pivotal. Nanomaterials with excellent electronic conductivity and high surface area, such as two-dimensional graphene-like nanomaterials, have emerged as promising energy receptors. Among these, MoS2 nanosheets (MoS2 NSs) are particularly promising due to their superior optical properties and water solubility (Lan et al., 2024). Notably, MoS2 NSs exhibit a high affinity for aptamers via van der Waals forces (Zhu et al., 2013). Shi et al. reported a sensor utilizing aptamer-modified quantum dots as donors and MoS2 NSs as acceptors for the detection of epithelial cell adhesion molecules (Shi et al., 2017). Bi et al. also combined CdTe quantum dot-labeled aptamers with MoS2 NSs to establish a FRET-based assay for OTA detection (Bi et al., 2022). However, most of these studies have focused on single-target detection. The simultaneous detection of multiple mycotoxins, such as OTA and AFB1, using a combination of carbon dots (CDs) and CdZnTe quantum dots (QDs) with MoS2 NSs in a FRET-based sensor has rarely been reported. Furthermore, these methods often require bulky instruments, limiting their application for rapid on-site analysis. Therefore, there is an urgent need for a portable, fast, and on-site analysis detection system.

The acceleration in the development of portable spectroscopic devices has been significantly propelled by the emergence of 3D printing technology (Jeong et al., 2021; Kim et al., 2020). Conventionally, portable spectroscopic devices incorporate ultraviolet diodes (UV-LEDs) and phototransistors into 3D-printed (De Lima, 2012) enclosures, creating fluorescence sensor devices with dark boxes. However, these devices are often limited by their inability to provide wavelength information, solely offering fluorescence intensity measurements (Kvittingen et al., 2017). This limitation can be effectively addressed by harnessing the embedded imaging sensors and computational power of smartphones, coupled with the spatial resolution of optical gratings (Bergua et al., 2022). Consequently, the current design leverages the robust computational capability of Raspberry Pi and the wavelength-resolving capabilities of the AS7265x multispectral sensor to achieve fluorescence spectral analysis.

The study introduced a fluorescent aptasensor, utilizing MoS2-modified bi-color QDs and a self-developed portable spectrometer, for rapid detection of OTA and AFB1. The key objectives of this research are threefold. Firstly, CDs and CdZnTe QDs were chosen as nanocarriers for bi-color quantum dots, specifically tagging aptamers for OTA and AFB1. These tagged aptamers were stably adsorbed onto the surface of single nanosheet MoS2 receptors, facilitating FRET. Notably, when the aptamers specifically bind to OTA and AFB1, the labeled aptamers attached to the nanocarriers are partially released, disrupting the FRET process and resulting in fluorescence recovery (Scheme 1A). Secondly, all changes in the dual-emission fluorescence can be captured using a portable spectrometer. This device integrates an AS7265x multispectral sensor, CMOS sensor, LEDs, and a Raspberry Pi within a 3D-printed enclosure. The serial port connects the Raspberry Pi and spectral sensor, enabling data collection and analysis through a PyQt5-based GUI application. Furthermore, the results can be uploaded to the cloud for advanced computing (Scheme 1B). Finally, this method has been validated in both laboratory settings and for analyzing natural occurrences of OTA and AFB1 (Scheme 1C). The developed host program interfaces with the spectrometer, transmitting data to the cloud, thereby imparting the device with Internet of Things (IoT) capabilities. Therefore, the combination of this sensor and portable fluorescent devices offers a convenient and reliable approach for detecting fungal toxins, advancing food safety measures.

OTA, deoxynivalenol (DON), zearalenone (ZEN), and AFB1 were obtained from Qingdao Pribolab Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The chemicals, including N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), MoS2 powder, and tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris), were purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Chemistry Reagents Co., Ltd. The apt sequences, 5′-NH2-GAT CGG GTG TGG GTG GCG TAA AGG GAG CAT CGG ACA-3′ (targeting OTA) and 5′-NH2-TGG GGT TTT GGT GGC GGG TGG TGT ACG GGC GAG GG-3′ (targeting AFB1), were synthesized by Shanghai Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. The apt-based sensor was prepared and tested using 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer under pH 7.4. All experiments were conducted with deionized water.

The synthesis of MoS2 employed a novel method known as “liquid phase sonication (Li et al., 2015) “. Initially, a flask with 40 mL of deionized water received an addition of 250 mg of bulk MoS2. Subsequently, the solution underwent stirring in a sealed flask at 700 rpm for 15 min. After stirring, the solution underwent continuous ultrasonication for 12 h in an ultrasonic bath. After the sonication process, the solution was carefully extracted from the flask and subsequently centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 25 min to effectively separate the collected supernatant liquid. The collected supernatant liquid was then freeze-dried to yield MoS2 powder. Ultimately, the resulting MoS2 NSs were scattered in 10 mM, pH 7.4 Tris-HCl buffer solution for subsequent applications.

CDs were synthesized according to a reported hydrothermal method (Yuan et al., 2016). First, add 0.4 g of sodium citrate, 3 g of ammonium bicarbonate, and 20 mL of water into a polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) stainless steel high-pressure reactor. The mixture is then treated at 180 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product (molecular weight cutoff at 1000 Da) is dialyzed against deionized water for 12 h to obtain pure CDs. The obtained CDs are stored at 4 °C for future use.

While CdZnTe QDs (Lu et al., 2019) were prepared following our previously reported procedure. In brief, the experimental procedure was as follows: firstly, 75.7 mg of NaBH4 and 51.1 mg of Te were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, and then the solution was bubbled with N2 gas and continuously stirred at 80 °C for 30 min to obtain NaHTe solution. Subsequently, a mixture of 182.7 mg of CdCl2·2.5H2O, 11 mg of ZnCl2, 160 mL of deionized water, and 144.4 mg of MPA was added into a three-necked flask, and the mixture was bubbled with N2 gas. The pH value was adjusted to 9 by dropwise addition of 1 M NaOH solution before the newly prepared NaHTe solution was added to the mixture. The resultant solution was bubbled with N2 gas for an additional 10 min, followed by transferring it into several Teflon-lined stainless steel high-pressure vessels (each with a volume of 25 mL) under N2 atmosphere. The high-pressure vessels were then heated in an oven at 190 °C for 35 min. After cooling to room temperature, the suspension underwent centrifugation in iron-fluoropolymer-lined stainless steel high-pressure vessels and was thoroughly washed with ethanol three times. Finally, CdZnTe QDs were successfully isolated and dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water for subsequent applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent bioprobes labeled with bi-color quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: To fabricate bioprobes, CDs conjugated with apt1 (QDs1-apt1) and CdZnTe QDs conjugated with apt2 (QDs2-apt2) for experimental applications were synthesized. Initially, 5 mL of the pre-prepared CDs solution with EDC (200 μM, 100 μL) and NHS (200 μM, 100 μL) solutions and stirred the mixture for 3 h to activate the carboxyl groups present on the quantum dots were combined. Subsequently, 20 μL OTA aptamer (apt1, 100 μM) were additionally mixed with the solution of CDs and stirred overnight under room temperature. The resulting QDs1-apt1 bioprobes were dissolved in 5 mL of Tris-HCl buffer and preserved under 4 °C. The preparation of QDs2-apt2 followed a similar protocol to the steps described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of aptasensors and detection procedures
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the efficacy of the fluorescence analysis, a comparative analysis was performed between the readings captured by the homemade portable spectrometer and the standard F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Specifically, 100 μL aliquots of the prepared bioprobes QDs1-apt1 and QDs2-apt2 were separately combined with 400 μL of MoS2 nanosheets (NSs), and the mixtures were incubated at room temperature for 10 min. Following this, the mixture was diluted to a final volume of 600 μL with Tris-HCl buffer. Next, the prepared aptasensors (QDs1-apt1/MoS2/QDs2-apt2) were incubated at room temperature with 20 μL of various concentrations of OTA and AFB1 for 35 min. Subsequently, the fluorescence intensity of the mixed suspensions was recorded for analysis. Both the homemade portable spectrometer and the F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer employed an excitation wavelength of 365 nm and a slit width of 10 nm, with a spectral range spanning from 380 nm to 750 nm. However, the homemade device featured a UV-light excitation wavelength of 365 nm and recorded values across 18 channels, ranging from 410 nm to 940 nm, in addition to fluorescence image response values. This comparative analysis allowed for a comprehensive evaluation of the performance of the homemade portable spectrometer in comparison to the industry-standard F-4500 instrument.

The instrumental characterization information including transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL 2100, Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra were studied using a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was performed from a Nicolet Nexus 470 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Nicolet, USA).

Corn samples were selected as real samples to test the detection ability of the suggested approach (Scheme 1C). Pretreatment was carried out using established methods (Zhou, Yao, et al., 2023). Briefly, 11 g of corn samples were thoroughly mixed and ground along with 1 g of NaCl, subsequently divided evenly into three portions of four grams each. OTA and AFB1 standards were precisely added to each aliquot of the sample powder and thoroughly mixed. Subsequently, these aliquots were combined with 10 mL of extraction solvent, consisting of a methanol-water mixture at a ratio of 6:4 (v/v), in a vortex shaker for a duration of 30 min. The mixture was then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min to eliminate any solid precipitate. After centrifugation, the maize samples were passed through a 0.45 mm syringe filter, and the pH was adjusted accordingly. Using Tris-HCl buffer, the concentration of OTA was set to 0.5, 1, and 5 ng·mL−1, while the concentration of AFB1 was adjusted to 0.5, 1, and 10 ng·mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and CdZnTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: To analyze the optical properties of CDs, UV–Vis spectra and fluorescence spectra were employed. The UV–Vis spectra revealed a prominent absorption peak at 340 nm (curve a, Fig. 1A). Under 365 nm excitation, the fluorescence spectra displayed a distinct emission peak centered at 442 nm (curve b, Fig. 1A). Visually, the CDs solution appeared colourless and transparent in ambient light (inset a, Fig. 1A), but emitted intense blue fluorescence when illuminated with 365 nm UV light (inset b, Fig. 1A). Subsequently, the morphology and size of the CDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM images revealed that the CDs exhibited an average diameter of approximately 5 nm (Fig. 1B). Turning to the CdZnTe QDs, the UV–Vis spectra showed an absorption peak at 590 nm (curve a, Fig. 1C), while the fluorescence spectra characterized an emission peak at 663 nm (curve b, Fig. 1C). Visually, the CdZnTe QDs exhibited light orange-red fluorescence in ambient light (inset a, Fig. 1C) and transitioned to red fluorescence under 365 nm UV illumination (inset b, Fig. 1C). TEM analysis further confirmed that the CdZnTe QDs possessed a spherical morphology with an average diameter of approximately 2 nm (Fig. 1D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of bioprobes labeled with bi-color quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Utilizing the interaction between amino and carboxyl groups, the aptamers were securely attached to the surfaces of quantum dots, respectively, aided by EDC/NHS (Wang et al., 2024). UV–Vis spectroscopy revealed that the unbound apt1 exhibited a distinct absorption peak at 260 nm (curve a, Fig. 1E), whereas the synthesized QDs1-apt1 conjugate displayed characteristic absorption peaks at both 260 nm and 347 nm (curve b, Fig. 1E). Additionally, the fluorescence emission peak of QDs1-apt1 was centered at 447 nm, exhibiting a 5 nm redshift compared to the emission peak of CDs (curve b, Fig. 1A). This shift can be attributed to the efficient conjugation of apt1 onto the surface of the CDs.

To validate the successful preparation of QDs2-apt2 bioprobes, UV–Vis spectra and fluorescence spectra analyses were performed. The apt2 (curve a, Fig. 1F) exhibited a distinct absorption peak at 260 nm, while the CdZnTe QDs (curve a, Fig. 1C) displayed a characteristic absorption peak at 590 nm. Notably, the QDs2-apt2 bioprobe displayed two absorption peaks, one at 260 nm and the other at 595 nm (curve b, Fig. 1F), respectively. The fluorescence spectra analysis revealed that the QDs2-apt2 bioprobe exhibited a strong emission peak centered around 669 nm, which exhibited a 6 nm red-shift compared to the emission peak of the CdZnTe QDs (curve c, Fig. 1F). This finding confirms the successful preparation of the QDs2-apt2 bioprobe.

The MoS2 NSs, prepared via the ultrasonic liquid-phase method, exhibited a distinct dark green hue (inset a, Fig. 1G). Their UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 1G, curve b, Fig. 1I) revealed four characteristic absorption peaks, specifically at 403 nm, 455 nm, 612 nm, and 671 nm (Chen et al., 2018; Li et al., 2015). The 403 and 455 nm peaks originate from direct exciton transitions from the deep valence band to the conduction band, while the 612 nm and 671 nm peaks correspond to the B and A exciton peaks, generated by K-points in the Brillouin zone of the two-dimensional MoS2 NSs with significant transverse dimensions (Wang et al., 2013). Moreover, TEM analysis (inset b, Fig. 1G) revealed that the MoS2 NSs exhibited planar sizes ranging from 50 nm to 100 nm, possessing a lamellar structure and excellent dispersion. This observation suggests that the exfoliation of bulk MoS2 into thinner forms favors molecular adsorption (Köster et al., 2022; Qian et al., 2024). Additionally, the FT-IR spectra of the MoS2 NSs (Fig. 1H) displayed characteristic absorption peaks. A peak near 490 cm−1 arises from MoS vibrations, while the peak at 3300 cm−1 is attributed to OH telescoping. Furthermore, characteristic absorption peaks at 1063 and 1469 cm−1 are due to SO telescopic vibrations (Song et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2016).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of fluorescent aptasensor and its reaction mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The constructed fluorescent aptasensor was based on bi-color FRET with MoS2 modification. The FRET phenomenon was generated when the fluorescence emission spectra of the donor quantum dots overlapped with the UV–Vis spectra of the acceptor MoS2 NSs, and at the same time the separation between the donor and the acceptor was within a range of fewer than 10 nm. To investigate the reaction principle, the mixture of QDs1-apt1 and QDs2-apt2 mixtures showed two emission peaks at 442 and 663 nm, and the difference between the two was able to drive the two emission peaks to be clearly shown (curve a, Fig. 1I). MoS2 was characterized using UV–Vis spectroscopy (curve b, Fig. 1I) and corresponding discussion can be found in section 3.3. Therefore, the absorption spectra of the two substances selected have a significant overlap and fulfil the conditions for FRET to occur.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection conditions and stability
ce:section.ce:para: Several experimental parameters were tuned to optimise the sensing system and ensure its best detection performance. First, the effect of the volume of MoS2 NSs on the FL intensity of the bioprobes QDs1-apt1 and QDs2-apt2 was explored. Therefore, a fixed volume of 100 μL of both bioprobes were combined with various volumes of MoS2 suspensions to investigate the impact of the volume ratio on the burst fluorescence. With the increasing use of MoS2 NSs, the fluorescence (FL) intensity initially decreased until reaching a stable level (Fig. 2A). Consequently, a volume ratio of 4:1 was determined as the optimal condition for inducing efficient bursting of fluorescence in CDs and CdZnTe QDs. Adsorption time between quantum dots and nanomaterials was optimized. The incubation time was optimized by combining 100 μL QDs1-apt1 (100 μL QDs2-apt2) and 400 μL MoS2 NSs over a period of 0 to 12 min. The FL intensity of the bio-complex exhibited a discernible reduction, reaching stabilization after a 10 min period (Fig. 2B). Therefore, a reaction time of 10 min was selected as optimal for both absorptions on the MoS2 surface.

Also, the reaction times between apt and toxin were important. OTA and AFB1 were added to the aptasensor, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded for various incubation durations, ranging from 0 to 40 min, at room temperature. As illustrated in Fig. 2C, the FL intensity of both bio-probes gradually increased in the first 35 min, and after 35 min, there was no change in the FL intensity of both curves. Therefore, the optimal incubation time was determined to be 35 min.

To evaluate the stability of the aptasensor, we conducted an in-depth analysis of its specificity in binding to its targets, along with its performance under various storage conditions and exposure to high-power UV irradiation. For the selectivity studies, two fungal toxins, DON and ZEN, were selected as common potential interferents and used at concentrations 10 times higher than OTA and AFB1. Notably, in the presence of these interfering toxins, the aptasensor exhibited no significant fluorescent signal. Conversely, when the aptasensor was coupled with OTA or AFB1, a substantial recovery of the fluorescent signal was observed (Fig. 2D). This further validates the aptasensor's high specificity and robustness. Additionally, we examined the sensor's stability under prolonged storage and continuous high-power UV irradiation (5.88 W, 40 °C, 3 h). Also, the matrices in cereals, food or feed are very complex. In this regard, a number of potentially coexisting mycotoxins and typical nutrients (e.g. glucose, vitamins and minerals) were used as interferents to assess the selectivity of the aptasensor (Fig. S1). Even when the concentration of interfering mycotoxins was 10-fold higher than that of OTA and AFB1, the intensity of the fluorescence response of the aptasensor to OTA and AFB1 was much higher than that of the tested interferents. The findings mentioned above indicated that the aptasensor exhibited excellent selectivity. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 2E, the FL intensity of the sensor remained at 98.7% on day 0 and retained 93.2% of its initial intensity even after 15 days of preservation. Similarly, as illustrated in Fig. 2F, the FL intensity of QDs1-apt1 (and QDs2-apt2) showed no significant variation under continuous high-power UV irradiation, thus demonstrating the robust stability of the aptasensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and manufacture of detection devices
ce:section.ce:para: The following was a schematic diagram of the detection principle of the set (Scheme 2A). First, the sample is excited by a UV LED to produce fluorescence in the cuvette. Then, the fluorescence is focused onto the multispectral sensor and the CMOS sensor to obtain the image and spectrum through a lens. The multispectral sensor converts the fluorescence signal to a digital signal and converts the voltage from 12 V buck to 5 V to feed the processor. The processor calculated the data and displays the detection resulted on an LCD screen. The composition of the portable spectra was illustrated in Fig. S2. In this, Raspberry Pi 4B was used as a controller and the AS7265x was employed as a spectral sensor for fluorescence detection within the spectral range of 410 nm to 940 nm, capable of discerning spectral values across 18 bands (Fig. S3). The spectral sensor is connected to the Raspberry Pi via the CH341T serial port, while the MOS sensor is connected via CSI. The display was controlled using a 5-in. capacitive touchscreen that was connected to the Raspberry Pi via a DSI. The light source module used a 365 nm micro-LED as the excitation light source and came with a copper heat sink on the bottom of the LED module base to ensure stability. The LED module was powered by a 5 V lithium polymer battery and used MOS tubes to regulate the voltage to the LEDs and PWM dimming to maintain consistent light intensity. The entire device used an XL4015 voltage regulator module to convert the 12 V Li-Polymer battery voltage to the 5 V required by the Raspberry Pi, and was equipped with a DC brushed fan to maintain the stability of the device. Finally, the overall form factor of the device was designed using Fusion 360 software and manufactured using a 3D printer. The circuit connections between the Raspberry Pi, the display control module, CMOS sensor, the spectra module, the power module, the LED module, the DC brushed fan and the battery were shown in Scheme 2B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneously fluorescence detecting OTA and AFB1
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimized conditions, the sensor's response to varying concentrations of OTA and AFB1 was thoroughly investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 3A, the degree of FL intensity recovery for the aptasensor gradually increased with the rising concentrations of OTA and AFB1. Notably, the FL intensity of the CDs (I 1) and CdZnTe quantum dots (I 2) exhibited a strong and linear correlation with the concentration of OTA (c 1) (curve a, Fig. 3B) and AFB1 (c 2) (curve b, Fig. 3B) over the range of 0.05–50 ng·mL−1. Specifically, the linear equation for OTA was I 1 = 282.83 + 150.29 lg [c 1 (ng·mL−1)], with an R 2 value of 0.9924, and a detection limit of 0.038 ng·mL−1 (S/N = 3). For AFB1, the equation was I 2 = 116.58 + 68.82 lg [c 2 (ng·mL−1)], with an R 2 of 0.9908 and a detection limit of 0.041 ng·mL−1 (S/N = 3).

As depicted in the inset of Fig. 3C, the fluorescence image response intensity escalated, with the color darkening progressively as the concentration of OTA and AFB1 increased. Separate extraction of the B and R channels from the image, as shown in Fig. 3C, revealed a clear upward trend in the values of both channels. Upon analysis, the B-channel response of fluorescence intensity (I a) exhibited a significant linear relationship with the concentration of OTA (c a) in the range of 0.5–25 ng·mL−1 (Fig. 3D, curve a). The linear equation was I a = 96.83 + 68.65 lg [c a (ng·mL−1)], with an R 2 value of 0.9883, and a detection limit of 0.057 ng·mL−1 (S/N = 3). Similarly, the R-channel response of fluorescence intensity (I
b ) showed a commendable linear relationship with the concentration of AFB1 (c b) in the same concentration range (Fig. 3D, curve b), following the linear equation I b = 56.24 + 72.38 lg [c b (ng·mL−1)], with an R 2 of 0.9875 and a detection limit of 0.064 ng·mL−1 (S/N = 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and implementation of control programs
ce:section.ce:para:
The FLAnalysis application, crafted with precision using PyQt5, aims to deliver an unparalleled user experience. Its core functionality, as illustrated in Scheme 2C, efficiently manages the LED switch, ensuring pinpoint illumination control. The AS7265x sensor subsequently receives the emitted signal and precisely plots the spectral curve for detailed analysis. Concurrently, the CMOS sensor captures the image and extracts RGB response values, which undergo further computations to generate and display a standard curve. A comprehensive flowchart detailing the software's operational process is presented in Fig. S4, S5, and S6. The graphical user interface (GUI), crafted with PyQt5, offers a user-friendly platform comprising three distinct sections (depicted in Fig. S7). Each section serves a specific purpose: one oversees LED control, another facilitates spectral curve visualization, and the third handles image analysis and RGB data display. The integration of these functionalities within a single, intuitive interface ensures a seamless experience for the end-user.
(1)
The system parameters encompassed a range of essential settings, including serial port configurations, spectral adjustments, and LED specifications. For serial port parameters, users could select the appropriate port and baud rate, establish a timeout period, and refresh the list of available serial devices. Connecting the sensor was simplified with a dedicated ‘Connect’ button. Spectral parameters allowed for the customization of integration time and precision, offering a choice between raw data and calibrated data formats. The range of precision parameters is X, 3.7X, 16X, and 64X, with higher values indicating higher accuracy. LED parameters facilitated the adjustment of frequency and duty cycle, and offered intuitive button controls for managing the LED switch.
(2)
The spectra were displayed in real-time, providing immediate feedback. By clicking the blank button, the system efficiently captured fluorescence blank values. Subsequently, activating the start button initiated the plotting of wavelength data curves, with corresponding numerical values for each wavelength displayed conveniently on the right side. The spectral operations offered a robust set of tools, including peak detection to identify the maximum value in the spectra, smoothing to refine the data and create a smoother curve, derivative analysis to reveal phenomena, and coloring to illustrate the color distribution within the visible wavelength band, assisting in visualizing areas of higher absorption. Users could select the eigenvalue button to obtain various fluorescence values, and these eigenvalues could be recognized and applied to subsequent test results for comprehensive analysis.
(3)
To conduct the test analysis, simply click the ‘Plot Standard Curve’ button to initiate the plotting process. Utilize the chosen eigenvalue as the Y-axis value, and input the tested concentration from the dialog box as the X-axis value. Add the curve's name to the Curve Library box and ensure it is checked to display the standard curve on the rightmost axis. Once completed, click the ‘Calculate Results’ button to accurately determine the value of the unknown concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Portable dual-mode fluorescence device for simultaneous detection of OTA and AFB1
ce:section.ce:para:
The spectra measured by the portable spectra were shown in Fig. 3E. The characteristic 435 nm and 680 nm were selected as the characteristic wavelengths. The analysis showed that the intensity (I a) at 435 nm exhibited a linear relationship with the concentration (c a) of OTA in the concentration range of 0.2–50 ng·mL−1 (curve a, Fig. 3F), with a linear equation of I a = 54.21+ 69.05 lg [c a (ng·mL−1)], R 2 = 0.9871, and the limit of detection was 0.045 ng·mL−1 (S/N = 3). The intensity at 680 nm (Ib) exhibited a linear relationship with the concentration of AFB1 (c b) over the concentration range of 0.2–50 ng·mL−1 (curve b, Fig. 3F), with a linear equation of I b = 42.33 + 59.81 lg [c b (ng·mL−1)], R 2 = 0.9898 with a detection limit of 0.048 ng·mL−1 (S/N = 3). The original image was measured as shown in the inset of Fig. 3G, where the concentration of OTA and AFB1 was increasing and the intensity of the color was deepening. The B and R channels of the image were extracted separately, as shown in Fig. 3G, and it can be clearly seen that the values of the R and B channels are increasing. By analysis, the R-response value of fluorescence intensity (I a) in the concentration range of 0.5–25 ng·mL−1 showed a linear relationship with the concentration of OTA (c a) (curve a, Fig. 3H), and the linear equation was I a = 28.73+ 73.28 lg [c a (ng mL−1)], R 2 = 0.9753, with a detection limit of 0.060 ng·mL−1 (S/N = 3). The R-response value of fluorescence intensity in the concentration range of 0.5–25 ng·mL−1 (I
b ) exhibited a linear relationship with the concentration of AFB1 (c b) (curve b, Fig. 3H), with a linear equation of I b = 76.17 + 87.05 lg [c b (ng mL−1)], R 2 = 0.9801, and the limit of detection was 0.068 ng·mL−1 (S/N = 3). The results of the portable dual-mode fluorescence device were comparable to the commercially available ones in the linear ranges of the assay comparable to that of the commercial one, which verified the reliability of the portable dual-mode fluorescence device for fluorescence detection. Comparison was made with the assays reported in the literature for OTA/AFB1 (Table 1 ). It can be seen that the developed sensor demonstrated a broad linear range for detecting the target, affirming the precision and dependability of the sensor.

To further validate the practicality of the sensor, it was used to detect OTA and AFB1 in corn samples. Samples were prepared with concentrations of OTA (0.5, 1, and 5 ng·mL−1) and AFB1 (0.5, 1, and 10 ng·mL−1). As seen from the Table S1, the measured recoveries in fluorescence spectra for the spiked OTA and AFB1 were within the ranges of 103.2–98.0% (RSD < 5.5%) and 104.2–97.1% (RSD < 6.6%), respectively. It showed that the aptasensor had better results in the spiked determination of OTA and AFB1. Meanwhile, as seen from the Table 2 , the recoveries of OTA and AFB1 in the portable dual-mode fluorescence device were in the range of 96.0–103.7% and 98.1–104.2%, with RSDs lower than 7.5% and 8.8%, respectively. It is shown that the use of this device combined with fluorescent aptasensor can be applied to the detection of real samples.

In this study, a fluorescent aptasensor based on MoS2 modified bicolor quantum dots for rapid detection of OTA and AFB1 was developed. Under optimized conditions, the linear range of the sensor in fluorescence spectral mode is (0.05–50 ng·mL−1), and the detection limits of OTA and AFB1 are 0.038 ng·mL−1 and 0.041 ng·mL−1, respectively. In fluorescence image mode, the linear range is 0.5–25 ng·mL−1, and the detection limits of OTA and AFB1 are 0.057 ng·mL−1 and 0.064 ng·mL−1, respectively. This method demonstrates the advantages in simple and selective determination of fungal toxins. In addition, we have developed a portable fluorescence detection and sensing platform using Raspberry Pi, AS7265x multispectral sensor, and CMOS sensor. This platform obtains fluorescence spectra and images through portable dual-mode fluorescence devices, achieving on-site quantitative detection of OTA and AFB1. The spectral detection range is 0.2–50 ng·mL−1, and the detection limits for OTA and AFB1 are 0.045 ng·mL−1 and 0.048 ng·mL−1, respectively. The developed host program is connected to the spectrometer and transmits data to the cloud, enabling the device to have Internet of Things (IoT) characteristics. Therefore, the combination of this sensor and portable fluorescent devices has the potential to detect fungal toxins, providing a convenient and reliable method for food safety.
